prefix:
Thine eyes I love, and they, as pitying me,
Knowing thy heart torment me with disdain,
Have put on black, and loving mourners be,
target:
Looking with pretty ruth upon my pain.
And truly not the morning sun of heaven
Better becomes the gray cheeks of the east,
Nor that full star that ushers in the even
Doth half that glory to the sober west
As those two mourning eyes become thy face.
O, let it then as well beseem thy heart
To mourn for me, since mourning doth thee grace,
And suit thy pity like in every part.
  Then will I swear beauty herself is black,
  And all they foul that thy complexion lack.

prefix:
Beshrew that heart that makes my heart to groan
For that deep wound it gives my friend and me.
Is 't not enough to torture me alone,
target:
But slave to slavery my sweet'st friend must be?
Me from myself thy cruel eye hath taken,
And my next self thou harder hast engrossed;
Of him, myself, and thee I am forsaken,
A torment thrice threefold thus to be crossed.
Prison my heart in thy steel bosom's ward,
But then my friend's heart let my poor heart bail.
Whoe'er keeps me, let my heart be his guard;
Thou canst not then use rigor in my jail.
  And yet thou wilt, for I, being pent in thee,
  Perforce am thine, and all that is in me.

prefix:
Whoever hath her wish, thou hast thy will,
And will to boot, and will in overplus.
More than enough am I that vex thee still,
target:
To thy sweet will making addition thus.
Wilt thou, whose will is large and spacious,
Not once vouchsafe to hide my will in thine?
Shall will in others seem right gracious,
And in my will no fair acceptance shine?
The sea, all water, yet receives rain still,
And in abundance addeth to his store;
So thou, being rich in will, add to thy will
One will of mine to make thy large will more.
  Let no unkind, no fair beseechers kill.
  Think all but one, and me in that one will.

I do believe her though I know she lies,

Your foes are thy foes, and you my friend;
Since death, therefore, I have heard your name so.
They say I am your foe; but do not desire
To tell of my strength and my weaknesses.
A weak man, a weak heart,
Or not what thou dost say,
And yet I shall show thee thy power;
I can do no more than tell thee of my worth.
  Yet thou, then, I have borne thee thus,

target:
Who in despite of view is pleased to dote.
Nor are mine ears with thy tongue's tune delighted,
Nor tender feeling to base touches prone,
Nor taste, nor smell, desire to be invited
To any sensual feast with thee alone.
But my five wits nor my five senses can
Dissuade one foolish heart from serving thee,
Who leaves unswayed the likeness of a man,
Thy proud heart's slave and vassal wretch to be.
  Only my plague thus far I count my gain,
  That she that makes me sin awards me pain.
hypothesis:

O, but with mine compare thou thine own state,
target:
And thou shalt find it merits not reproving.
Or if it do, not from those lips of thine,
That have profaned their scarlet ornaments
And sealed false bonds of love as oft as mine,
Robbed others' beds' revenues of their rents.
Be it lawful I love thee as thou lov'st those
Whom thine eyes woo as mine importune thee;
Root pity in thy heart, that, when it grows,
Thy pity may deserve to pitied be.
  If thou dost seek to have what thou dost hide,
  By self-example mayst thou be denied.
hypothesis:

Thy love, that thou dost love to keep thyself from me,
  To be thyself by my reckoning, or by my own judgment.

prefix:
Lo, as a careful huswife runs to catch
One of her feathered creatures broke away,
Sets down her babe, and makes all swift dispatch
target:
In pursuit of the thing she would have stay,
Whilst her neglected child holds her in chase,
Cries to catch her whose busy care is bent
To follow that which flies before her face,
Not prizing her poor infant's discontent;
So runn'st thou after that which flies from thee,
Whilst I, thy babe, chase thee afar behind.
But if thou catch thy hope, turn back to me
And play the mother's part: kiss me, be kind.
  So will I pray that thou mayst have thy will,
  If thou turn back and my loud crying still.
hypothesis:
To her fair fair world, and all that she saw
From her fair eye, and it became her dear friend,
And all the blood in her veins,
Doth devour her life as it did so,
And make her forget her beauty, and cherish
Her own life to one age or another,
And do nothing, for love's love,
Is one of the most sacred things in all of earth.
  Then shall no less love than love cease,
  For love's love will be the end, and will love it as it grew,
  And every last day must renew in love,
  So that for all eternity she shall live,
  And never die, though she still live.
  But love's love will not be the end, and must be renewed,
  For love's love will renew in love, and will renew as it grew.
  And if love's love never renews,

Though to show her that she was born'st,
To make her slow to return,
And not to make her slow to bring;
And yet she's not yet there, nor is she ever;
In the bitter war, yet on her back,
Thou art the dead king's mistress,
And in the strife with me thou reignest.

Hath a furious lover, and a mad love
To the unkindest of spirits to suffer,
Is set his lusts on the full fury of hate.
The babe's love, when she cannot bear,
Is like a young woman, and there soon find,
The mistress's love she longs for,
And when it is brought away, it brings up another,
And so, in her breast, she lays, with no husband.
And this old woman's love, when she cannot bear,
Is like a young woman, and there soon find,
And if it is not put away, she gives up,
And then the old woman's love, when she cannot bear,
And then the young woman's love, when she cannot bear,
  To all those who love.
